message:
perf(fsqlite-btree/cursor): reuse scratch buffers for DELETE defrag path (OPT-A4)

remove_cell_from_leaf runs on every DELETE whose target cell sits on a
leaf page (the common case). It re-reads the cell-pointer array and
builds a temporary (ptr, size, idx) triple array to plan the
defragmentation compaction. Both vectors were previously freshly
allocated on every call, producing two allocator round-trips per
delete in a hot path.

This commit adds two cursor-owned scratch buffers —
`defrag_ptrs_scratch: Vec<u16>` and
`defrag_cells_scratch: Vec<(usize, usize, usize)>` — that are taken
out via std::mem::take, filled, and put back before the function
returns. Vec capacity is preserved across the take/put cycle, so
after a few large deletes the scratch capacity stabilizes and
subsequent deletes pay zero allocator calls for the ptr/cell arrays.

Implementation notes
  - Uses the existing `cell::read_cell_pointers_into(&mut Vec<u16>)`
    API (which clears + reserves its caller's buffer) rather than
    `read_cell_pointers`, which returns a fresh Vec.
  - Error paths carefully restore the scratch buffers before
    returning so their capacity is not lost on a mid-defrag failure
    (database-corruption bailout or cell-operation error).
  - N * 24 bytes (3 usize per cell) saved per delete on the
    cells-to-move allocation alone; the ptr array saves N * 2 bytes.
  - BtCursor::new populates both with empty Vec::new() so the first
    delete on a fresh cursor allocates once and then reuses.

No behavioral change; this is a pure throughput/latency optimization
covered by existing DELETE conformance + regression tests.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
